items.text: Translations in this thesis are taken from the NRSV and NRSVue, unless otherwise noted. In this case, the translation is noted as my own.
Luke 12:35: “ Ἔστωσαν ὑμῶν αἱ ὀσφύες περιεζωσμέναι⸃ καὶ οἱ λύχνοι καιόμενοι.”
Tashio Aono, Die Entwicklung des paulinischen Gerichtsgedankens bei den apostolischen Vätern (EHS.T 137; Bern, Frankfurt, and Las Vegas: Peter Lang, 1979), 178.
For a full discussion of the ages of the characters, see the exposition in chapter 3.

O’Loughlin, The Didache, 133. It appears to echo a common biblical phrase found in both the Septuagint and New Testament (gird your loins), though less clearly, yet “it probably implies the same imagery as that in Luke 12:35.” Niederwimmer and Attridge, The Didache, 215. Niederwimmer remarks that: “Αἱ ὀσφύες ὑμῶν μὴ ἐκλυέσθωσαν is less clear than the analogous biblical expressions in Exod 12:11*
Jer 1:17*, and elsewhere in the Jewish Scriptures, and then in Luke 12:35*

For a full discussion of the earliest reception history of this parable post-dating the Didache’s reference, particularly with reference to the Epistula Apostolorum, Gospel of Thomas, and the artwork at Dura-Europas, see chapters 4 and 6.
For a deeper discussion regarding the evaluation of the actions of the foolish virgins, see chapter 5.
See a deeper discussion regarding the evidence for the foolish virgins successfully getting their oil in chapter 5.

It is likely though that, at the very least, the author of the Didache agreed with the Matthean parable that the foolish virgins were indeed foolish for their lack of preparation (however he imagined the circumstances of how that came about), given that he notes “you should be ready because you do not know the hour at which our Lord is coming.” Quoting the translation given by O’Loughlin, The Didache, 133. Yet, despite this, he curiously does not place the emphasis on the oil as later interpreters come to do, but the flame itself.
Peter Yaw Oppong-Kumi, Matthean Sets of Parables (Tübingen, Germany: Mohr Siebeck, 2013), 359.

Jan Lambrecht, Out of the Treasure: The Parables in the Gospel of Matthew (Louvain Theological & Pastoral Monographs Book 10; Louvain: Peeters, 1998), Kindle Edition. “Matthew has also composed the introduction of verse la. This is evident through the use of ‘then,’ the expression ‘the kingdom of heaven’ and the passive-deponent form of the verb homoioo (‘to compare,’ here in the sense of ‘will be like’). The future of the same verb also occurs in 7:24 and 26 where, in all probability, it must also be ascribed to Matthew. Whether or not the evangelist substituted this formula for another introduction can no longer be established.”
Add sources
Ulrich Luz, Matthew 21–28, 231. “The original parable, whose wording we are not able to reconstruct, may come from Jesus.”

Some scholars have argued that the version of the parable found in the second-century Epistula Apostolorum represents closer the original version of the parable that pre-dated Matthew. From this perspective, the parable was adapted and changed in various ways through its early reception. As will be argued in chapter 6, however, this proposal seems unlikely and a more probable assumption would be that the Epistula has rewritten the Matthean parable (or a similar oral form of the parable) to avoid Matthew’s more negative depictions of the wise virgins. Thus, those like Ulrich Luz argue that “We have no versions that are independent of the Matthean story.” Ibid., 231.
To borrow Amy-Jil Levine’s description of them. See

Rosenblatt lamented this, noting that “What shall account for the fact that the story of the ten virgins and their lamps doesn't receive major attention in recent books on parables?” Rosenblatt, “Got into the Party after All,” 174. She argues that one explanation “is its liturgical invisibility, like other scriptural narratives about women which don't appear in the lectionary.” Writing in the 90s, she noted that based on the then current Roman Catholic Lectionary, “it is read once every three years for the Sunday Eucharist, but may not be heard for six years.” Ibid., 175. Academically, the lack of interpretive diversity she posits is due to the “oddness” of the story, noting that “the parable of the ten virgins has resisted all but the most conventional readings.
For example, the following list of popular works on the parables represent the standard introductions for this material. Among them, not one chapter reviews the Parable of the Ten Virgins.
For example, Amy Jill-Levine’s celebrated work on the parables ignores this Matthean text as well.

For example, see Epiphanius’ comment (Interpretation of the Gospels 36) that explicitly references this verse: “ ‘As the bridegroom was delayed, they all slumbered and slept. But at midnight there was a cry, “Behold, the bridegroom! Come out to meet him.” ‘ Likewise, the blessed apostle, Paul, declared, ‘For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the archangel’s call and with the sound of the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first
then we who are alive, who are left, shall be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air
and so we shall always be with the Lord.’ ” Ibid., 219.
Nikos Kazantzakis, The Last Temptation of Christ (trans. P. A. Bien; New York: Simon & Schuster, 1960), 217.
Information taken from IMDB. https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0167234/?ref_=ttep_ep_10 [accessed Nov. 12, 2024].

One cannot help but notice that the five wise virgins are all presented with light and fair skin, whereas three of the five foolish virgins are presented as darker colored Palestinians. It is hard to know what to make of this odd feature of the animation, or who is to blame for this apparently racially insensitive depiction: director, animators, etc.?
See chapter 7 and its discussion of scholarly attempts to reconstruct the historical background of the parable.

Sara Parks has argued that this is something seen in the earliest stratum of material found in the Q source and that this may indicate that the Historical Jesus regularly presented such parallels between men and women.

J. Massingberd Ford, “The Parable of the Foolish Scholars (Matt. xxv I-I3),” Novum Testamentum 9 (1967), 107. See also Rosenblatt: “When compared with the trend of the treatment of women in the Gospels and women's overall relationships with Jesus, the polemical aspect of the parable stands out in relief. What is consistent with a gospel portrait of Jesus and women is the kingdom-bridegroom parable which presents women in an attractive and inspiring light. What is inconsistent with Matthew's own portrayal of women throughout the rest of the Gospel is the suggestion that the Lord rejects half the community of women in the wisdom-foolishness polemic.” She believed that something needed to explain “for the simultaneous presence of a friendly and an unfriendly portrayal of women.” Rosenblatt, “Got into the Party after All,” 172.

Rosenblatt notes that: “A feminist reading points up the alienating features of the parable and raises suspicions about the purpose for it in Matthew’s Gospel.” Rosenblatt, “Got into the Party after All,” 172. She also proposes that the Bridegroom represents a potentially negative evaluation of women. “One interpretation for the delay, favorable to the bride, has proposed that the delay concerns the complicated negotiations over the price of the bride, to be paid by the groom as compensation to the bride's family. She is very valuable in her father’s eyes, so the bargaining is protracted. But this perspective cuts both ways. It could also mean that the groom, ready to marry, is unwilling to pay the original price to the bride's family. They are all delayed because they are caught in a bind over his unwillingness to pay a price he doesn't think she’s worth, and their desire to marry her off, even at a bargain price.” Rosenblatt, “Got into the Party after All,” 184.
Jan Lambrecht, Out of the Treasure: The Parables in the Gospel of Matthew (Louvain Theological & Pastoral Monographs Book 10; Louvain: Peeters, 1998), Kindle Edition.
